title: The model is absorbing the harness — so don't build for lock-in
summary: Two Google voices argue the harness around an agent matters less over time as the model absorbs scaffolding, and that betting on a proprietary harness for lock-in is the wrong bet.
takeaways: Treat your harness as scaffolding the model will eventually absorb: build it to be swappable, not as a moat.
When you must triage where effort goes, domain expertise and a good harness can outweigh chasing the single best model.
Models that gain capability through post-training shrink the work your harness has to do.

sources.title: Logan Kilpatrick: Why the Model Eats the Harness
Cloud CISO Perspectives: 4 lessons that guided AI Threat Defense

body:
## What happened

The harness is everything wrapped around a model to make it useful in production: tool-calling (letting the model invoke functions), code execution, and the agent container that holds a running session. In an interview [published 2026-06-11](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cMAs8z2dehs), Logan Kilpatrick — who runs Google AI Studio and the Gemini API at Google DeepMind — argued that "the model eats the harness": scaffolding you build by hand keeps getting absorbed into the model itself. His conclusion for builders is blunt — building a proprietary harness *for lock-in* will matter less as models grow general enough to adapt to any harness. He points to a Gemini 3.5 Flash model that, he says, beats prior Pro models on coding purely through [post-training](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cMAs8z2dehs) (extra training after the base model, no new harness).

## Why it matters

If you bet your architecture on a clever harness as a durable advantage, the next model release can quietly erase that advantage. The cost is sunk engineering: glue code, custom tool wrappers, and container logic that the model now does natively. The defensible work moves up the stack — to vertical expertise and the parts a model cannot internalize.

## How it works

1. **Scaffolding migrates inward.** Capabilities like tool-calling and code execution start as harness code, then ship inside the model.
2. **Post-training closes gaps.** Kilpatrick cites a Flash model surpassing older Pro models on coding through [post-training alone](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cMAs8z2dehs).
3. **Triage when forced.** Google Cloud CISO Chris Betz, in a [2026-06-15 post](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/cloud-ciso-perspectives-the-4-lessons-that-guided-ai-threat-defense/), describes vulnerability-finding as a product expert plus a harness plus a model — and says if forced to pick two, [pick expertise and harness over the best model](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/cloud-ciso-perspectives-the-4-lessons-that-guided-ai-threat-defense/).
4. **Keep the harness swappable.** Treat it as replaceable plumbing so a better model drops in cheaply.

> Build the harness to be discarded — the moat is your domain knowledge, not your glue code.

## What broke

The failure mode is building a harness as a moat. Betz's [AI Threat Defense framework](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/cloud-ciso-perspectives-the-4-lessons-that-guided-ai-threat-defense/) shows the durable version: the edge comes from business context and expertise attackers lack, not from any single model — and the harness is one swappable input among three. The fix is to invest harness effort in the layer the model won't absorb: your vertical knowledge and the operational scaffolding (token and hardware budgets, human review, rollback) around it.

[Harness Engineering](/guide/harness-engineering/)
